title: Botox Brow Lift Injection Patterns. Botox Mark-Up Tutorial
filename: Botox Brow Lift Injection Patterns. Botox Mark-Up Tutorial.en.vtt
excerpt: So Layla expressed to us that she'd noticed these little horizontal lines that for her age are a bit more in your perception than her friends and this is what is causing those horizontal lines which is the frontalis muscle full name is the occipital frontalis muscle because it ac…

segments.text: so Layla expressed to us that she'd noticed these little horizontal lines that for her age are a bit more in your perception than her friends and this is what is causing those horizontal lines which is the frontalis muscle full name is the occipital frontalis muscle because it actually comes all the way from the back of the head and reaches around although that's kind of interesting it doesn't really affect what we do mostly there are enough adhesions that I think it moves independently you don't often see the whole scalp moving and we want to relax as much of this muscle as we can without losing the support for the eyebrow particularly in a female if you cause that drop they're very unhappy you can actually get away with it with men much more often because firstly that I put iron Shadow on if you ask someone you'll know this but for the men watching if you apply eyeshadow you instinctively lift your eyebrows to do it and that's the thing that women complain about they can actually look okay at neutral so when you look at them they don't look like they've got a brow drop but they're very upset by the fact they can't move their eyebrows so that really helped me

earlier on to realize it's not just about the appearance it's about the function of the muscle and to leave some muscle moving as much as you can in most situations um right next to it right inferior to it you have the next muscle we're going to be concerned about which is the corrugated muscle starts deep on the bone and moves its way as it crosses laterally to insert into the surface of the skin usually just near the mid-pupil or slightly lateral from it and you can see the insertion point which is fibers directly connected with the surface of the skin when someone does a really big frown can you see if we can do that for us yeah so this just here is where the insertion point is anything lateral to that is not going to make any difference to the frown line but it does have a chance of affecting the frontalis muscle particularly if you're a little bit on the deeper side and that is going to cause asymmetry to your browse so this is a very important point to know about we need to be very superficial and low dose here not to just to protect for eyelid ptosis because the levator pulping muscle is

very close you can see that on this model here that's the muscle we worry about with um droopy eyelid but it's actually the frontalis muscle that causes more problems so if you go too lateral you get asymmetry to the eyebrows if you've done it on one side and not the other and you can also get heaviness to the eyebrow so it's a very important point to isolate know that you see the insertion point and then inject I usually say five millimeters medial to that insertion point a couple of other interesting muscles so the procerus muscle which pulls down here not everyone has a very strong one so let's see if you can show us your proceris if you yeah you can see there's some downward pull not particularly strong I'd say the Layla mostly she's pulling in this direction corrugated with a little downward pull you certainly don't need to put seven units into that which a lot of people do you probably do two units and you get the same result then you have the depressor supercelli which some people consider just a branch of orbicular succulent it's a slight downward and lateral pull that does this when you frown if you do a big frown again and whether it's there or not it's kind

of academic we're basically treating in the same place as the corrugator the belly of the corrugator is in the same spot so if we treat that sufficiently we'll get those fibers as well okay orbicularis oculi um it has an origin medially and connections with the lateral facial ligaments here the lateral chemical thickening here is ligaments as attachment to the Skin So when you're that muscle it contracts it tends to contract respecting where it's attached so um if you think of it what you when this normally comes to play when you're treating someone with botulin toxin is that if you relax the inferior part the medial part will still pull towards its attachment and you get this strange pull in some people in this direction so we need to be aware that it's in a tug of war but a circular tug of war wherever you relax it you're going to get a pull often in the other direction particularly with the other muscles now this isn't always a bad thing because we actually use it for eyebrow lifts if you relax this muscle here if you close your eyes very tightly you'll see what happens to your eyebrows eyebrow gets pulled down

pulled down and these muscle fibers here are pulling the eyebrow downwards which means if we relax them you're going to get the frontalis muscle unopposed able to lift slightly more so this tug of war will be in our favor if we relax here it's not enough either because we pull immediately the levator pulpberry muscle runs all the way to the back of the globe and its strongest portion is well within the globe behind the orbital septum so the orbital septum is a very thick if you see it on a cadaver one day it's really quite as tough structure it's not that easy for something to permeate through which tells me most of the eyelid ptosis are come are coming through being too deep you've either gone through the orbital septum or maybe there's some abnormality there or with the orbital septum which is also possible or maybe even through the little foramen above because that's how you get into the globe so depth is very important and if you don't do that you should enjoy many years without getting an eyelid ptosis so if you could do a big frown for me now just as we're talking about I'm trying to see where the insertion points are and I

might you might in the beginning Mark that out because it's lateral to that his no muscle that we need that we're interested in right there is the actual insertion point but this is probably the safer place to inject because it just means you're further from the lateral frontalis you then I then go to the deepest and strongest part of the muscle so I'm trying to predict where the strongest part is and I'd say that's probably there and then I might do the process if you wrinkle up that so we'll put a little injection in there as well and it's basically repeating the same finding insertion point a little bit medial to it finding the heaviest and deepest part so next thing is the forehead is actually the most difficult to mark up because it's one of the areas where it's most clear that we're in a balancing act because I need some muscle to move and some muscle to be relaxed so I won't get a difference and so most of your follow-ups are forehead follow-ups because you have too much movement or too little movement you're either correcting for a bit of heaviness or you're trying to correct for too much movement into living lines but it helps

to draw out the the box that you know is safe to treat in and this is my way of describing that and there are lots of other ways you'll come across but I like to try and base it on this principle that I want relaxed muscle and working muscle so lower down I want a bit of movement and higher up I don't want much movement I also want a trend towards more movement laterally because she's female so I'd rather there was more movement here than I wouldn't be the end of the world but I don't want heaviness of the sides lifting the middle so in terms of preventing heaviness in the brow it's about two centimeters or in a very small forward about fifty percent of the forehead from the orbits that I leave untreated so for you to mark that on approximately using this pencil where does the orbit start so good question you can feel for that it's very close to the underside of the eyebrow so lay this case you can actually feel just where our eyebrow starts is where the orbit is so two centimeters above that should leave enough muscle functioning

functioning I also then follow the Contour of the forehead so it's not a dead straight line if I start with two centimeters here and follow around that'll give me a good idea of where that safety zone is and you can follow this all the way around and so that's an area where I've aside from where we're injecting here which you can box off and we're not going to inject the other areas let's call it a higher risk area not a no-go area that sometimes you do end up going there but as a first pass patient you would probably not treat there you're looking for areas where there is no muscle movement that you can basically ignore so it might it's not a large area but a little area like that where there's an aponeurosis it might go down the middle of the forehead some people think it does some people think it doesn't um but broadly speaking the muscle does have two bellies to it and in the middle is the weakest so I tend not to put an injection right in the middle so I can see this muscle kind of ending here as

well so as this tape is off so I want to elevate the eyebrows based around where the arch of the eyebrow is and so what I'm trying to get to happen is I want the muscle fibers that are directly pulling in that direction to be left untreated and as active as possible and so the way I do that is I draw a line um following where the eyebrow art should be you can draw a line from the base of the nose through the eye bit of the eye and then up here and it's like you can think of that as a vector this is the direction that you want the muscle to pull so once you've got the direction we then need to figure out how much muscle to leave untreated because if you leave muscle untreated that is not part of this Vector you end up with a ladder line so say this whole area here lateral is untreated then she will get lines that run all the way up side of her forehead so I wanted to taper off I'm trying to leave as little muscle as possible to do the lifting While most of the lines fade and the way I've learned to do that over

the years is to start with the base of the eyebrowser measurement and choose the same amount each distance apart which is approximately where that is where the Mercury line is and then you can draw and line it mainly lateral messes about the media line because electrical to this is no muscle and this becomes another boundary this is where I don't want to treat if I want eyebrow lift maximum that's a key word if I want maximum eyebrow lift which there was not super maximum plus super high in your case because your forehead is smaller on a big forehead you can get ridiculous eyebrow lifts but I still might soften that but that's just giving me my first boundary but the concept here once you're treating this part of the forehead is to fill with enough injections that you reduce the strength of the muscle evenly throughout and so I would normally start right up against the boundary remembering that each injection actually spreads about 1 to 1.5 centimeters so if I inject there I'm actually it's more like a finger sized injection

so if I do that all the way along how I draw all the circles but essentially that's what I'm imagining my mind if these injections and I can fill the space and you can fill it any way you want so let's move on to orbicularis oculi so this muscle very superficial under the skin is about 0.2 millimeters we don't need to be deep the Deep you go the more you get the side effects lateral rectus tear duct and the one I forgot to mention earlier is the zygomatic muscle never seen someone actually get that but if you go deep straight through cheek fat then you hit the origin of the zygomaticus major and they'll get an asymmetrical smile miserable Okay so I'm picturing Now sort of Ray-Ban shape muscle that splays around the whole eye and if you ask her to smile you can often see the contraction of that bulge here so that's actually where my most effective injections will go I usually start tailor the eyebrow pretty safe place to start actually it's more

complex as you go inferior lateral canthus and then just near the cheek at near the lateral the cheek Junction and then I'll do a couple of minor doses here it sends check everything and just check that it looks broadly symmetrical and you haven't calculated something incorrectly just like with your remember GCSE maths dude always my teacher always would say now look at the number and say does it actually make sense yeah it's a bit like them okay [Music]
